text:
MEDICAL ONCOLOGY FOLLOW-UP NOTE
Patient: Robert Delacroix | DOB: [on file] | MRN: [on file]
Attending: [Oncologist on file]
Visit type: Established patient follow-up — Lutetium-177 PSMA-617 Cycle 2 Post-Treatment Assessment

---

CHIEF COMPLAINT
Return visit for assessment after completion of cycle 2 of Lu-177 PSMA-617 (vipivotide tetraxetan, Pluvicto) for metastatic castration-resistant prostate cancer (mCRPC). Evaluation of PSA response, hematologic status, tolerability, and symptom burden.

---

HISTORY OF PRESENT ILLNESS
Mr. Delacroix is a 72-year-old male with a well-documented history of high-risk metastatic prostate adenocarcinoma (Gleason 9, Grade Group 5; original staging cT3bN1M1b) initially diagnosed at age 68, now presenting with widely metastatic castration-resistant disease. His treatment history is extensive: he progressed through ADT + abiraterone/prednisone (approximately 18 months, excellent initial PSA response to nadir 0.8 ng/mL, then CRPC transition), enzalutamide (progression at 9 months with new bone metastases including T10/T12 with epidural extension requiring palliative EBRT), and then olaparib 300 mg BID (dose-reduced to 200 mg BID for grade 3 anemia) — on which he had an initial PSA response (nadir 1.4 ng/mL) but subsequently progressed at approximately 8 months with new hepatic and pulmonary metastases. Germline BRCA2 pathogenic variant (c.5946delT) with confirmed somatic LOH drove olaparib eligibility; liver biopsy at progression confirmed metastatic prostatic adenocarcinoma without neuroendocrine transdifferentiation. Repeat NGS from liver met showed AR-V7 positivity, high-level MET amplification, and progressive AR amplification — establishing expected resistance to further ARSI therapy and indicating a non-hormone-driven biology. PSMA PET/CT at progression showed widespread PSMA-avid disease including hepatic, pulmonary, and osseous sites, confirming eligibility for lutetium-177 PSMA-617.

He received cycle 1 of Lu-177 PSMA-617 at 7.4 GBq IV approximately 6 weeks ago and cycle 2 at 7.4 GBq IV approximately 2 weeks ago. He tolerated cycle 2 without acute infusion-related events. He received amino acid infusion (arginine/lysine) with each cycle for salivary gland cytoprotection per institutional protocol. He presents today for post-cycle 2 laboratory and clinical reassessment.

Current symptoms reported by patient:
- Dry mouth: persistent since cycle 1, grade 1 xerostomia, manageable with frequent sips of water and sugarless gum; not affecting oral intake significantly
- Fatigue: grade 2, somewhat improved from the nadir of his olaparib-related fatigue period, though still limiting — requires afternoon rest periods, difficulty with sustained physical activity
- Appetite: subjectively improved compared to the 3-4 weeks prior to starting Lu-177; wife reports he is eating small but regular meals
- Back pain: improved; thoracic back pain (T10/T12 region) that was grade 2 at the time of enzalutamide progression is now grade 1 and managed with acetaminophen 650 mg TID PRN
- No new neurological symptoms, no bowel or bladder changes, no fevers/chills
- Mild lower extremity edema bilateral (longstanding, grade 1)
- No bleeding, no melena, no hematuria
- No nausea or vomiting since cycle 1 (was grade 1 post-infusion, self-limited)

He continues on concurrent ADT (leuprolide acetate 22.5 mg IM q3 months — last injection 5 weeks ago), zoledronic acid 4 mg IV q12 weeks (last dose 3 weeks ago), calcium 1200 mg daily, vitamin D 1000 IU daily, amlodipine 5 mg daily for hypertension, and acetaminophen PRN. He is actively followed by palliative care co-management for symptom burden and goals-of-care planning. Goals-of-care conversation has been initiated with patient and family, consistent with focus on quality of life and time at home.

---

REVIEW OF SYSTEMS
Positive: fatigue (grade 2), dry mouth (grade 1), mild bilateral leg swelling, mild back pain (grade 1, improved), decreased appetite (improved from prior visit). 
Negative: no fever, no chills, no night sweats, no dyspnea at rest (dyspnea on exertion has improved vs. olaparib period), no chest pain, no hemoptysis, no abdominal pain, no nausea, no vomiting, no diarrhea, no constipation, no dysuria, no hematuria, no new neurological deficits, no headache, no visual changes, no skin rash.

---

PHYSICAL EXAMINATION
Vital signs: BP 138/82 mmHg, HR 74 bpm (regular), RR 16/min, SpO2 96% on room air, Temperature 36.8°C, Weight 85 kg (down 6 kg from pre-olaparib baseline of 91 kg; weight appears to have stabilized — was 84.5 kg at cycle 1 post assessment).
General: Alert, oriented x3. Appears mildly fatigued but conversant and engaged. In no acute distress.
HEENT: Mild oral mucosal dryness consistent with xerostomia. No oral mucositis. No cervical lymphadenopathy.
Chest: Clear to auscultation bilaterally. No dullness to percussion. No wheezing.
Cardiovascular: Regular rate and rhythm. No murmurs. No JVD.
Abdomen: Soft, non-tender, non-distended. No hepatosplenomegaly appreciated on palpation. No ascites.
Musculoskeletal: Mild mid-thoracic paraspinal tenderness to deep palpation at T10-T12 level (consistent with known metastatic disease, improved from prior assessment). No focal bony tenderness in pelvis or right femur on exam today.
Neurological: No focal deficits. Strength 5/5 bilaterally in upper and lower extremities. Gait somewhat cautious but steady with use of single-point cane. No signs of myelopathy.
Extremities: 1+ bilateral pitting edema to the ankles. No erythema or warmth.
Skin: No rash, no petechiae, no ecchymoses.
Lymph nodes: No peripheral lymphadenopathy on exam.

---

LABORATORY RESULTS
(Drawn today, or most recently available)

PSA: 11.2 ng/mL
  [Pre-Lu-177 baseline (post-olaparib progression): 18.4 ng/mL → cycle 1 assessment: 14.8 ng/mL → today: 11.2 ng/mL; cumulative 39% decline from baseline]

Testosterone: 7 ng/dL (confirmed castrate, consistent with ADT on board)

CBC:
  - Hemoglobin: 8.6 g/dL (grade 2 anemia; baseline at Lu-177 start was 8.4 g/dL post-transfusion)
  - Hematocrit: 26.2%
  - WBC: 4.1 x10^9/L (mild decrease from pre-Lu-177 baseline 6.2 x10^9/L; no neutropenia — ANC estimated ~2.3 x10^9/L)
  - Platelets: 142 x10^9/L (decreased from 198 x10^9/L at baseline; grade 1 thrombocytopenia, no clinical bleeding)
  - Reticulocytes: 1.1% (mildly low-normal)

Comprehensive metabolic panel:
  - Creatinine: 1.2 mg/dL, eGFR ~62 mL/min/1.73m2 (stable chronic kidney disease stage 2-3a, no acute change)
  - BUN: 22 mg/dL
  - Sodium: 138 mEq/L, Potassium: 4.1 mEq/L
  - ALT: 52 U/L (grade 1, mildly elevated — was 48 U/L at olaparib progression; may reflect hepatic metastatic burden)
  - AST: 47 U/L
  - Total bilirubin: 0.9 mg/dL (normal)
  - Alkaline phosphatase: 195 U/L (mildly elevated, down from 210 U/L at olaparib progression — potentially early response in osseous/hepatic disease)
  - Albumin: 3.4 g/dL (mildly low; nutritional status a consideration)

LDH: 218 U/L (mildly elevated)

PT/INR: 1.1 (normal)

---

IMAGING RESULTS
No new cross-sectional imaging at this visit. CT restaging is planned following completion of cycle 4 per protocol. Most recent relevant imaging:
- PSMA PET/CT (Ga-68 PSMA-11) at olaparib progression: widespread PSMA-avid disease with hepatic (at least 4 lesions, max SUV 12.4), bilateral pulmonary, osseous (thoracic spine, lumbar spine, pelvis, right femur), and nodal (right obturator, external iliac) involvement — this scan confirmed robust PSMA expression and patient eligibility for Lu-177 PSMA therapy.
- CT C/A/P at olaparib progression: hepatic lesions in segments 5 (1.8 cm) and 7 (1.2 cm), bilateral pulmonary nodules (RLL 8 mm, LLL 6 mm), progressive lymphadenopathy and stable-to-worsening osseous disease.
Imaging to be repeated after cycle 4.

---

ASSESSMENT
1. Metastatic castration-resistant prostate adenocarcinoma (mCRPC), Gleason 9 (4+5), germline BRCA2 pathogenic variant (biallelic loss confirmed), AR-V7 positive, widely metastatic (bone, liver, lung, pelvic nodes). Post-abiraterone/prednisone, post-enzalutamide, post-olaparib (Lynparza) — now cycle 2 of Lu-177 PSMA-617 (Pluvicto).
   - PSA declining from baseline 18.4 to current 11.2 ng/mL (39% decline after 2 cycles) — early biochemical response. The kinetics of PSA decline with Lu-177 PSMA can lag radiographic response; PSA flare in early cycles is also possible, but the progressive decline here is encouraging.
   - Alkaline phosphatase trending down slightly (210 → 195 U/L), which may represent early bone/liver disease response, though this remains speculative at this timepoint.

2. Anemia, grade 2, chronic — multifactorial (prior olaparib-related myelosuppression, now ongoing Lu-177 hematologic effects, bone marrow replacement by metastatic disease). Hemoglobin 8.6 g/dL, stable from baseline. No acute transfusion indication today. Platelets at 142 x10^9/L — grade 1 thrombocytopenia, watching trend.

3. Xerostomia, grade 1 — expected toxicity from Lu-177 PSMA-617 salivary gland exposure. Managing conservatively. Good salivary gland cytoprotection with amino acid infusion used with each cycle.

4. Renal function: eGFR ~62 mL/min, stable. Monitoring given Lu-177 renal excretion. Adequate hydration encouraged.

5. Nutritional status: mild hypoalbuminemia (3.4 g/dL), weight loss total ~6 kg over prior 3 months (appears stabilized). Appetite has improved per patient and family report. Palliative care nutrition support ongoing.

6. Pain: grade 1 thoracic back pain, well-controlled on scheduled acetaminophen. If worsening, would escalate analgesia through palliative care team.

7. Goals of care: patient and family engaged, understand incurable nature of disease. Focus on quality of life and maximizing time at home. Patient currently declining cytotoxic chemotherapy (docetaxel, cabazitaxel). Supportive of continuing Lu-177 series given tolerability and early PSA response.

---

PLAN
1. Lu-177 PSMA-617: Proceed with cycle 3 in approximately 4 weeks, pending CBC recovery and renal function stability. Dose to remain 7.4 GBq. Continue amino acid cytoprotection infusion with each cycle. Anticipate 6 cycles total per VISION protocol, with reassessment at cycle 4.

2. Hematologic monitoring: Repeat CBC in 2 weeks. Transfusion threshold hemoglobin <8.0 g/dL (or symptomatic). Platelet threshold for Lu-177 cycle 3: will require platelets >75 x10^9/L and ANC >1.5 x10^9/L per protocol. If thrombocytopenia worsens, may need cycle delay or dose modification. Continue to defer erythropoiesis-stimulating agents given uncertainty in oncologic context — re-discuss if transfusion burden increases.

3. Renal monitoring: Repeat BMP/creatinine in 2 weeks and at pre-cycle 3 visit. Encourage fluid intake of at least 2L/day to promote Lu-177 urinary clearance and limit renal exposure.

4. ADT: Continue leuprolide 22.5 mg IM q3 months. Next scheduled injection in ~7 weeks. Testosterone to be rechecked at next visit to confirm ongoing castrate levels.

5. Bone health: Continue zoledronic acid 4 mg IV q12 weeks, calcium, and vitamin D. Next scheduled Zometa dose in ~9 weeks. Monitor dental hygiene — jaw pain screening performed today (negative). Ongoing osteonecrosis of the jaw precautions in effect.

6. Symptom management: Continue acetaminophen 650 mg TID PRN for back pain. If pain escalates to grade 2 or above, will escalate through palliative care team — consider low-dose opioid titration. Dry mouth: recommend biotene products, frequent hydration, sugarless xylitol candies.

7. CT restaging (chest/abdomen/pelvis with contrast) planned after cycle 4, approximately 8 weeks from now. Will consider repeat PSMA PET/CT if CT response is ambiguous or for treatment decision-making after cycle 6.

8. Palliative care: Continue active co-management. Next palliative care appointment scheduled concurrently. Advance directive reviewed today — in place and up to date.

9. Patient education: Reviewed Lu-177 radiation safety precautions (reviewed written materials, patient/wife verbalize understanding — limiting close prolonged contact for 48 hours post-infusion, separate bathroom use for 24-48 hours). Reviewed signs/symptoms requiring urgent contact: fever >38°C, significant bleeding, new neurological symptoms, severe pain, dyspnea.

10. Return to clinic: In 2 weeks for CBC check, then pre-cycle 3 visit in 4 weeks.

Elapsed time with patient and family: 45 minutes. Reviewed treatment goals, response assessment, toxicity monitoring, and near-term plan.

— [Attending Oncologist, MD]
[Electronically signed]
